---
title: "Harnessing Large Language Model Engineering for Automated Systematic Reviews: A Multi-Agent Pipeline with PRISMA 2020 Self-Auditing"
author:
  - name: Hsieh-Ting Lin
    orcid: 0009-0002-3974-4528
    affiliations:
      - name: Koo Foundation Sun Yat-Sen Cancer Center
        department: Department of Oncology
        city: Taipei
        country: Taiwan
    corresponding: true
    email: htlin222@gmail.com
date: "2026-03-26"
format:
  pdf:
    toc: false
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
    cite-method: citeproc
    documentclass: article
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    fontsize: 11pt
    linestretch: 2.0
    keep-tex: true
    include-in-header:
      - text: |
          \usepackage{tikz}
          \usetikzlibrary{positioning, calc, shapes.geometric}
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
  docx:
    toc: false
    number-sections: true
bibliography: references.bib
csl: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/citation-style-language/styles/master/american-medical-association.csl
abstract: |
  **Background:** Systematic reviews represent the highest level of evidence in clinical decision-making, yet conventional workflows require 6--18 months and substantial expert labor. Existing automation tools such as ASReview and Rayyan address title-and-abstract screening but leave the remaining pipeline---full-text appraisal, data extraction, narrative synthesis, and reporting---entirely manual. No current tool produces submission-ready manuscripts with verifiable PRISMA 2020 compliance.
  
  **Methods:** We developed an open-source, end-to-end systematic review pipeline orchestrated by large language models. The system executes multi-database search across Scopus, PubMed, and Embase with automated deduplication; filters journals by quartile ranking using Scimago and OpenAlex (Q1 restriction); and validates every DOI against the Crossref API. Structured data extraction employs a Claude Haiku model with field-level schema enforcement to capture study design, population characteristics, interventions, and quantitative outcomes. Manuscript generation uses eight parallel modular writing agents, each responsible for a discrete PRISMA-aligned section. A PRISMA 2020 self-audit module evaluates all 27 checklist items and triggers an automated repair loop for any non-compliant element. Nine human-in-the-loop checkpoints are embedded at critical junctures---search strategy approval, inclusion decisions, extraction verification, and final manuscript review---to preserve expert oversight. The pipeline was validated on three hematology/oncology topics: hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH), diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL), and multiple myeloma (MM).
  
  **Results:** The pipeline produced three complete, journal-formatted manuscripts. HLH comprised 12,755 words with 35 of 36 PRISMA sub-items satisfied; DLBCL comprised 10,636 words (35/36); and MM comprised 10,934 words (33/36). Haiku-based structured extraction captured quantitative outcome data in 70% of included articles compared with 42% using regular-expression baselines. All DOIs achieved 100% validation against Crossref. Comparison with independently published expert reviews demonstrated comparable clinical granularity in reported outcomes and treatment recommendations.
  
  **Conclusions:** Large language model engineering enables end-to-end systematic review automation with built-in quality assurance through PRISMA self-audit and human-in-the-loop governance. The pipeline reduces time-to-manuscript from months to days while maintaining methodological rigor. The tool is freely available as open-source software on GitHub.
  
  **Registration:** This study was not registered as it describes a methodological tool rather than a specific systematic review.
  
  **Keywords:** systematic review automation, large language model, multi-agent orchestration, PRISMA 2020, evidence synthesis, human-in-the-loop, structured extraction
---

{{< include sections/01-background.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/02-methods.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/03-results.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/04-discussion.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/05-conclusions.qmd >}}

# Declarations

**Ethics approval and consent to participate:** Not applicable. This study describes a methodological tool and does not involve human participants, human data, or human tissue.

**Consent for publication:** Not applicable.

**Competing interests:** The author declares no competing interests.

**Funding:** This research received no external funding.

**Authors' contributions:** H-TL conceived and designed the pipeline, implemented the software, conducted the validation experiments, and wrote the manuscript.

**Availability of data and materials:** The pipeline source code, all prompt templates, extracted data, and generated manuscripts for all three case studies are publicly available at https://github.com/htlin222/robust-lit-review under the MIT license. The Scimago journal ranking data used for quality filtering is available at https://www.scimagojr.com/.

**Registration:** This study was not registered as it describes a methodological tool rather than a specific systematic review.

# References

::: {#refs}
:::
